rulings.Case Title: RAMON GOMEZ VS. ZERS DEVELOPMENT, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Cross-Defendant City Of South San Francisco?S Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Declaring Lack Of Good Faith And Reasonable Cause In Bringing Proceeding Under Tort Claims Under Ccp 1038 And An Award Of Post Ccp 998 Costs
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, May 19, 2021, Line 1, CROSS DEFENDANT CITY OF SOUTH SAN FRANCISCO'S Motion For Order Declaring Lack Of Good Faith And Reasonable Cause In Bringing Proceeding Under Tort Claims Under CCP 1038 And An Award Of Post CCP 998 Costs: The Court found in its April 27, 2021 order that Cross-Defendant City of South San Francisco is entitled to recover its attorney's fees and other costs incurred in defending Defendants and Cross-Complainants Luen Shee Shek and Shek Construction's cross-complaint from August 24, 2018 to the present. Under Code of Civil Procedure section 1038, the City is entitled to "all reasonable and necessary defense costs, in addition to those costs normally awarded to the prevailing party." Section 1038 defines defense costs to include "reasonable attorney's fees," as well as expert witness fees and other expenses "where reasonably and necessarily incurred in defending the proceeding." (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1038(b); see also, e.g., Austin B. v. Escondido Union School District (2007) 149 Cal.App.4th 860, 888.) Where, as here, the moving party provides the court with "detailed time records and a declaration from counsel establishing the defense costs incurred," plus "a breakdown of fees incurred commencing from various points of time," such a showing is sufficient to support a motion for defense costs under section 1038. (Suarez v. City of Corona (2004) 229 Cal.App.4th 325, 338.) [The Court's full tentative ruling was emailed to the parties.] Finally, Shek's request that the Court continue the hearing to allow the City's fees to be reviewed by an expert witness is denied. The City's fees motion was filed on March 22, 2021. Shek had ample time to retain such an expert, if it wished to do so. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the City is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Felicia Houston'S Demurrer To Plaintiff'S Second Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, May 19, 2021, Line 7, 2. Defendant Felicia Houston'S Demurrer To Plaintiff'S Second Amended Complaint: Defendant Felicia Houston's demurrer to the second amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend. The Court has not considered Plaintiff's untimely "supplemental opposition," nor her declarations, which improperly rely upon matters outside the pleadings. It is unclear what allegations Defendant Houston is being asked to respond to or what claims are asserted against her. The only allegations referring to her in the lengthy second amended complaint that the Court has been able to locate that have any factual content at all appear on pp. 13 and 26, where plaintiff alleges that on March 6, 2020, Houston, who allegedly managed the Woman's Place clinic and shelter, cancelled plaintiff's reservation. (SAC pars. 40, 77.) In any event, all of plaintiff's claims appear to seek recovery for alleged personal injuries, which are governed by a two-year statute of limitations (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 335.1). As noted, the SAC arises out of plaintiff's discharge on January 5, 2017 from a medical care facility. The original complaint was not filed until April 5, 2019, and plaintiff did not name Houston as a defendant until August 22, 2019, when she filed the first amended complaint. As a result, any claims she may have against Houston are time-barred. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for Houston is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ELOY ROBLES VS. JACK STEPHENSON ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash 24 Subpoenas Served On Defendants Healthcare Providers And For Monetary Sanctions Against Plaintiff And His Attorney
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, May 19, 2021, Line 3, DEFENDANT JOHN STEPHENSON's Motion To Quash 24 Subpoenas Served On Defendants Healthcare Providers And For Monetary Sanctions Against Plaintiff And His Attorney. Pro Tem Judge Peter Catalanotti, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link (DISCOVERY, DEPT 301 @ 9:00AM), or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to Peter.Catalanotti@wilsonelser.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If parties have arranged for a court reporter to appear, please include the following in your email: (1) ensure that they are California Certified Shorthand Reporters; (2) the reporter's full contact information [name, CSR number, personal work email, and phone number]. If the reporter is not a California Certified Shorthand Reporter, the transcript cannot be certified for use in California courts. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must e-mail it to the Judge Pro Tem. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JULIANNA AGARDI VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, May 19, 2021, Line 3, 1. DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT: Defendant the City and County of San Francisco's demurrer to plaintiff Julianna Agardi's first amended complaint (FAC) is sustained without leave to amend. Plaintiff asserts that her FAC is filed "with the purpose of enforcing plaintiff's judgment" as to case number CGC-10-500534. (FAC para. 25.) However, in that case, this court granted the City's judgment on the pleadings, dismissed the case with prejudice and entered judgment in favor of the City. The Court of Appeal affirmed this court's judgment. Contrary to plaintiff's apparent misimpression, no judgment was ever entered in her favor. There can be only one final judgment in an action. In the present FAC, plaintiff again alleges the City is violating its obligation to pay General Assistance and is defrauding plaintiff. (FAC paras. 25, 28, 38, 39.) The action is barred by law of the case and/or by res judicata. (Morohoshi v. Pacific Home (2004) 34 Cal.4th 482, 491 ["The decision of an appellate court, stating a rule of law necessary to the decision of the case, conclusively establishes that rule and makes it determinative of the rights of the same parties in any subsequent retrial or appeal in the same case"]; Association of Irritated Residents v. Department of Conservation (2017) 11 Cal.App.3d 517, 530-531 [res judicata prevents the relitigation of the same causes of action between the same parties].) Plaintiff contends that the City's citation to the Court of Appeal's prior opinion is improper. Plaintiff is wrong. (Cal. R. Ct. 8.1115(b)(1) [an unpublished opinion may be cited or relied on when it is relevant under the doctrines of law of the case, res judicata, or collateral estoppel].) As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the City is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: RAYMOND V. DIGIACOMO JR. VS. COMCAST CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, May 19, 2021, Line 6, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT; Defendant City and County of San Francisco's demurrer to the first amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend. Plaintiff alleges that defendant Comcast Corporation provides "internet-oriented services and infrastructure to residents and/or commercial entities" in San Francisco, and that it has negligently maintained its CATV internet cabling boxes by failing to ensure that their covers are fastened, such that vehicles driving over those boxes generate "gunshot-like" noise that disturbs plaintiff. (FAC paras. 6-8.) Plaintiff asserts that his complaints to the City's Department of Public Works have gone unanswered, and that the City has not provided him with contact information for Comcast's service manager. (Id. paras. 13-15.) He asserts a variety of claims against the City, including negligence, dangerous condition of public property, violation of an asserted mandatory duty, violation of Article I, section 1 of the California Constitution, and "relief from illegal expenditure" under Code of Civil Procedure section 526a. Plaintiff seeks unspecified "injunctive/equitable relief." However, Plaintiff pleads no facts and cites no authority supporting the proposition that the City can be vicariously or jointly and severally liable for Comcast's alleged negligence in failing to maintain its CATV cable boxes. [The Court's full tentative ruling was emailed to the parties.] Because Plaintiff has failed to show a reasonable likelihood of amending the complaint to overcome the foregoing defects and allege a cognizable claim, the City's demurrers are sustained without leave to amend. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
